did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid AI pipeline for early skin cancer detection on the ISIC-2020 dataset. The method involves Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) clustering for lesion segmentation, a pretrained Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for deep feature extraction, and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) for classifying Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, and Keratosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents a novel hybrid classification pipeline (CLAHE-enhanced FCM-CNN-SVM) for early skin cancer detection using the ISIC-2020 dataset. It details original methodology, simulation results, performance metrics (91.89% accuracy), and comparative analysis against baseline models, which are characteristics of an original experimental study rather than a review.

title: CLAHE-Enhanced FCM-CNN-SVM Pipeline for Early Skin Cancer Detection Using ISIC-2020 Dataset
journal: 2025 International Conference on Engineering Innovations and Technologies (ICoEIT)
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid pipeline for early skin cancer detection using the ISIC-2020 dataset, focusing on classifying Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, and Keratosis. The methodology integrates Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for preprocessing, Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) clustering for lesion segmentation, and a fusion of handcrafted (GLCM, color, shape) and deep features extracted via a pretrained CNN. These combined features are classified using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) with a Radial Basis Function kernel. The model was evaluated against baseline U-Net and traditional CNN architectures using standard performance metrics. The proposed approach achieved a classification accuracy of 91.89%, outperforming U-Net (87.6%) and traditional CNNs (86.2%). The results demonstrate the effectiveness of combining enhanced preprocessing, precise segmentation, and hybrid feature fusion for robust dermatological diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three clinically significant classes selected from the ISIC-2020 dataset for training and evaluation. Used in segmentation (Fig. 2) and classification tasks.
One of the three clinically significant classes selected from the ISIC-2020 dataset for training and evaluation. Used in segmentation (Fig. 3) and classification tasks.

One of the three clinically significant classes selected from the ISIC-2020 dataset for training and evaluation. Referred to as 'Keratosis' in methodology and results, and 'Seborrheic Keratosis' in Figure 4 caption. Used in segmentation (Fig. 4) and classification tasks.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories: Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, and Keratosis
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly describes a 'hybrid classification pipeline' for 'early skin cancer detection'. The model predicts discrete class labels (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Keratosis) using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as Accuracy (91.89%), Precision, Recall, and F1-score, along with a Confusion Matrix and ROC curves for class separation.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2020
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing dermoscopic images of various types of skin lesions, used in this study for early skin cancer detection.

use.training: Used to train the hybrid classification pipeline (CLAHE-enhanced FCM-CNN-SVM).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the model's performance, achieving 91.89% accuracy.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: GLCM features (Contrast, Energy, Homogeneity, Entropy, Correlation)
Color features (Mean and standard deviation of RGB and HSV channels)

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using absolute accuracy values (e.g., 91.89% vs 87.6% vs 86.2%) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC-2020.notes: The proposed method outperformed baseline models U-Net and traditional CNN. The confusion matrix (Fig. 5) and ROC curves (Fig. 6) were used for evaluation. Table 1 provides specific GLCM texture feature values (Contrast, Energy, Homogeneity, Entropy, Correlation) for six representative images across the three classes.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained CNN model used for deep feature extraction (specific architecture name not reported)

architecture_details.segmentation: Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) clustering for lesion segmentation and Region of Interest (ROI) extraction

handcrafted_features: GLCM features (Contrast, Energy, Homogeneity, Entropy, Correlation)
Color features (Mean and standard deviation of RGB and HSV channels)
feature_extraction.deep_features: High-dimensional abstract features extracted via pretrained CNN

architecture_details.fusion: Concatenation of handcrafted features and deep CNN features to form a unified feature vector

preprocessing_techniques: Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for enhancing local contrast and highlighting skin lesion regions without over-amplifying noise
Median Filter for noise reduction to remove salt-and-pepper noise while preserving edge features
Image Normalization using min-max scaling to adjust pixel intensity values to a [0, 1] range
conclusions: The proposed CLAHE-enhanced FCM-CNN-SVM hybrid pipeline achieves a classification accuracy of 91.89% on the ISIC-2020 dataset for detecting Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, and Keratosis.
The proposed method outperforms baseline U-Net (87.6% accuracy) and traditional CNN models (86.2% accuracy) in terms of segmentation quality and classification accuracy.

The integration of Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for preprocessing and Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) clustering for segmentation improves lesion visibility and segmentation robustness under variable image quality conditions.
Fusing handcrafted features (GLCM, color, shape) with deep features extracted via a pretrained CNN enhances predictive performance compared to using either feature type alone.
The study acknowledges limitations regarding generalizability across diverse datasets, lack of cross-dataset validation, and limited interpretability for clinicians.
The current model is not optimized for real-time deployment in clinical or mobile environments and lacks evaluation against adversarial examples or spoofing attacks.

Future work will focus on optimizing computational efficiency for edge deployment, expanding the model to support multimodal dermatological imaging, and integrating with electronic health records for real-time clinical applications.
important_remarks: The study lacks cross-dataset validation, relying solely on the ISIC-2020 dataset, which limits the assessment of generalizability to diverse clinical populations.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of the performance difference between the proposed model (91.89%) and baselines (U-Net 87.6%, CNN 86.2%).
The specific architecture of the pretrained CNN used for feature extraction is not reported, hindering reproducibility.
The paper does not report dataset split details (training/validation/testing counts), raising potential concerns about data leakage or non-representative splits.
The model is not optimized for real-time deployment or edge devices, and computational efficiency metrics are not provided.
The study does not evaluate robustness against adversarial examples or spoofing attacks, which poses a risk for practical clinical deployment.
The 'Keratosis' class likely corresponds to Seborrheic Keratosis, but the paper uses inconsistent terminology ('Keratosis' vs 'Seborrheic Keratosis'), which may cause ambiguity.
The model acts as a 'black box' with limited interpretability for clinicians, as noted in the limitations regarding clinical adoption.
